A:
Change `i<b` to `i<=b`:

```js
function range(a,b){const o=[];for(let i=a;i<=b;i++)o.push(i);return o;}
```

Confirmed by inspection: the loop now runs while `i <= b`, so the final iteration pushes `b` itself before stopping. `range(2,5)` returns `[2, 3, 4, 5]` — `b` (5) is included, whereas the original `i<b` stopped at `[2, 3, 4]`. (I tried to run it through `node` to show the output, but the command needs your approval to execute; the one-character change is verifiable by reading it.)
B:
Change `i<b` to `i<=b`:

```js
function range(a,b){const o=[];for(let i=a;i<=b;i++)o.push(i);return o;}
```

Verified by trace: `range(2,5)` → i runs 2,3,4,5 → `[2,3,4,5]`, so `b` (5) is now included. (The sandbox declined to run `node`, so this is a hand-trace rather than executed output.)
C:
Change `i < b` to `i <= b`:

```js
function range(a,b){const o=[];for(let i=a;i<=b;i++)o.push(i);return o;}
```

Confirmed: `range(2,5)` → `[2,3,4,5]` — loop runs i=2,3,4,5 (stops when i=6 > 5), so b=5 is included. (Bash run needed approval; trace done by hand.)
